Bubble-sort
(cid:73) Basic principle:
(cid:73) (i, j) with i < j is an inversion if a[i] > a[j]
(cid:73) While there is an inversion (i, i + 1) interchange a[i] with a[i + 1]
(cid:73) Algorithm:
Procedure bubbleSort(a, n)
begin
last ← n − 1
while (last > 0) do
n1 ← last–1; last ← 0
for i ← 0 to n1 do
if (a[i] > a[i + 1]) then
swap(a[i], a[i + 1])
last ← i
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
5 / 44

Bubble sort
(cid:73) Analysis
(cid:73) Worst-case performance
a[0] > a[1] > ... > a[n − 1]
Time for searching: O(n − 1 + n − 2 + ... + 1) = O(n2)
TbubbleSort(n) = O(n2)
(cid:73) Best-case performance: O(n)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8

Insertion sort
(cid:73) Basic principle:
suppose a[0..i − 1] sorted
insert a[i] such that a[0..i] becomes sorted
(cid:73) Algorithm (search sequentially the position of a[i]):
Procedure insertSort(a, n)
begin
for i ← 1 to n − 1 do
j ← i–1 // a[0..i − 1] sorted
temp ← a[i] // search the place of temp
while ((j ≥ 0) and (a[j] > temp)) do
a[j + 1] ← a[j]
j ← j–1
if (a[j + 1]! = temp) then
a[j + 1] ← temp
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
9 / 44

Insertion sort
(cid:73) Example
3 7 2 1
3 7 2 1
2 3 7 1
1 2 3 7
(cid:73) Analysis
(cid:73) searching the position i in a[0..j − 1] needs O(j − 1) steps
(cid:73) worst-case scenario a[0] > a[1] > ... > a[n − 1]
Search time: O(1 + 2 + ... + n − 1) = O(n2)
TinsertSort(n) = O(n2)
(cid:73) best-case scenario: O(n)

Selection sort
(cid:73) Apply the following scheme:
(cid:73) the current step: select an element and bring it on the ﬁnal position in
the sorted table;
(cid:73) repeat the current step until all elements reach the ﬁnal positions.
(cid:73) After the type of the selection of an element:
(cid:73) Naive selection: choose elements in the order they are initially (from
n − 1 to 0 or from 0 to n − 1)
(cid:73) Systematic selection: use max-heap
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
12 / 44

Selection sort (naive selection)
(cid:73) In the order n − 1, n − 2, ..., 1, 0, namely:
(∀i)0 ≤ i < n =⇒ a[i] = max{a[0], ..., a[i]}
Procedure naivSort(a, n)
begin
for i ← n − 1 downto 1 do
imax ← i
for j ← i − 1 downto 0 do
if (a[j] > a[imax]) then
imax ← j
if (i! = imax) then
swap(a[i], a[imax])
end
(cid:73) The time complexity for all cases is O(n2)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019

The operation of insertion in heap
Procedure insertTth(a, n, t)
begin
j ← t
heap ← false
while ((2 ∗ j + 1 < n) and not heap) do
k ← 2 ∗ j + 1
if ((k < n − 1) and (a[k] < a[k + 1])) then
k ← k + 1
if (a[j] < a[k]) then
swap(a[j], a[k])
j ← k
else
heap ← true
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
17 / 44

Heap sort
Procedure heapSort(a, n)
begin
// build the max-heap
for t ← (n − 1)/2 downto 0 do
insertTth(a, n, t)
// remove
r ← n − 1
while (r > 0) do
swap(a[0], a[r ])
insertTth(a, r , 0)
r ← r − 1
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
18 / 44

(cid:80)k−1
(cid:73) remove from heap and redo the heap
i=0 2i2i = (k − 2)2k+1 + 4
(cid:80)k−1

Divide-et-impera paradigm
(cid:73) P(n): problem of dimension n
(cid:73) base:
(cid:73) if n ≤ n0 then solve P by elementary methods
(cid:73) divide-et-impera:
(cid:73) divide P in a problems P1(n1), ..., Pa(na) cu ni ≤ n/b, b > 1
(cid:73) solve P1(n1), ..., Pa(na) in the same way and obtain the solutions
S1, ..., Sa
(cid:73) assemble S1, ..., Sa to obtain the solution S to the problem P
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
23 / 44

Paradigma divide-et-impera: algorithm
Procedure DivideEtImpera(P, n, S)
begin
if (n ≤ n0) then
ﬁnd S by elementary methods
else
Divide P in P1, ..., Pa
DivideEtImpera(P1, n1, S1)
...
DivideEtImpera(Pa, na, Sa)
Assemble(S1, ..., Sa, S)
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
24 / 44

Merge sort
(cid:73) generalization: a[p..q]
(cid:73) base: p ≥ q
(cid:73) divide-et-impera
(cid:73) divide: m = [(p + q)/2]
(cid:73) subproblems: a[p..m], a[m + 1..q]
(cid:73) assembling: interclass the sorted subsequences a[p..m] ¸si a[m + 1..q]
(cid:73) initially store the result of interclassing in temp
(cid:73) copy from temp[0..q − p + 1] in a[p..q]
(cid:73) complexity:
(cid:73) time: T (n) = O(n log n)
(cid:73) additional space: O(n)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
25 / 44

Interclassing two sorted sequences
(cid:73) the problem:
(cid:73) given a[0] ≤ a[1] ≤ · · · ≤ a[m − 1], b[0] ≤ b[1] ≤ · · · ≤ b[n − 1],
construct c[0] ≤ c[1] ≤ · · · ≤ c[m + n − 1] such that
(∀k)((∃i)c[k] = a[i]) ∨ (∃j)c[k] = b[j]) and for k! = p, c[k] and c[p]
come from diﬀerent elements
(cid:73) the solution
(cid:73) initially: i ← 0, j ← 0, k ← 0
(cid:73) the current step:
(cid:73) if a[i] ≤ b[j] then c[k] ← a[i], i ← i + 1
(cid:73) if a[i] > b[j] then c[k] ← b[j], j ← j + 1
(cid:73) k ← k + 1
(cid:73) termination criterion: i > m − 1 or j > n − 1
(cid:73) if it is the case, copy in c the elements from the unﬁnished table
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019

Quick sort
(cid:73) generalization: a[p..q]
(cid:73) base: p ≥ q
(cid:73) divide-et-impera
(cid:73) divide: ﬁnd k between p and q by interchanges such that after ﬁnding
k we have:
(cid:73) p ≤ i ≤ k =⇒ a[i] ≤ a[k]
(cid:73) k < j ≤ q =⇒ a[k] ≤ a[j]
(cid:73) subproblems: a[p..k − 1], a[k + 1..q]
(cid:73) assembling: none
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
28 / 44

Quick sort: partitioning
(cid:73) initially:
(cid:73) x ← a[p] (may choose x arbitrarily from a[p..q])
(cid:73) i ← p + 1; j ← q
(cid:73) the current step:
(cid:73) if a[i] ≤ x then i ← i + 1
(cid:73) if a[j] ≥ x then j ← j − 1
(cid:73) if a[i] > x > a[j] si i < j then
swap(a[i], a[j])
i ← i + 1
j ← j − 1
(cid:73) termination:
(cid:73) the condition i > j
(cid:73) operations
k ← i − 1
swap(a[p], a[k])
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
29 / 44

Quick sort: partitioning - example
Procedure partitioning(a, p, q, k)
begin
x ← a[p]
i ← p + 1
j ← q
while (i <= j) do
if (a[i] <= x) then
i ← i + 1
if (a[j] >= x) then
j ← j − 1
if (i < j) and (a[i] > x) and (x > a[j])
then
swap(a[i], a[j])
i ← i + 1
j ← j − 1
k ← i − 1
a[p] ← a[k]
a[k] ← x
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
30 / 44

Quick sort: recursion - example
Procedure quickSort(a, p, q)
begin
while (p < q) do
partitioning (a, p, q, k)
quickSort(a, p, k − 1)
quickSort(a, k + 1, q)
end
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019

Counting sort
(cid:73) Assumption: a[i] ∈ {1, 2, . . . , k}
(cid:73) Find the position of each element in the sorted table by counting how
many elements are lower than him
1 Procedure countingSort(a, b, n, k)
2 begin
3
for i ← 1 to k do
c[i] ← 0
for j ← 0 to n − 1 do
c[a[j]] ← c[a[j]] + 1
for i ← 2 to k do
c[i] ← c[i] + c[i − 1]
for j ← n − 1 downto 0 do
b[c[a[j]] − 1] ← a[j]
c[a[j]] ← c[a[j]] − 1
4
5
6
7
8
9
10
11
12 end
Complexity: O(k + n)

Distribution sort
(cid:73) Assumption: the elements a[i] are uniformly distributed over the
interval [0, 1)
(cid:73) Principle:
(cid:73) divide the interval [0, 1) in n subintervals of equal sizes, indexed from 0
to n − 1;
(cid:73) distribute the elements a[i] in the corresponding interval: (cid:98)n · a[i](cid:99);
(cid:73) sort each package using another method;
(cid:73) combine the n packages in a sorted list.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
39 / 44

Distribution sort
(cid:73) Algorithm:
Procedure bucketSort(a, n)
begin
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
insert(B[(cid:98)n · a[i](cid:99)], a[i])
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
sort the list B[i]
concatenate in order the lists B[0], B[1], · · · , B[n − 1]
end
The average complexity: O(n)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019

When a sorting algorithm is favourite?
(cid:73) A sorting method is stable if it keeps unchanged the relative order of
elements with identical keys
(cid:73) Recommendations
(cid:73) Quick sort: when you don’t need a stable method and the average
performance is more important than the worst one; O(n log n) - the
average time complexity, O(log n) auxiliary space
(cid:73) Merge sort: when a stable method is required; time complexity
O(n log n); drawback: O(n) auxiliary space, a larger constant than
QuickSort
(cid:73) Heap sort: when you don’t need a stable method and you are
interested more in the worst case performance than the average one;
time O(n log n), space O(1)
(cid:73) Insert sort: when n is small
FII, UAIC
Lecture 8
DS 2018/2019
43 / 44

When a sorting algorithm is favourite?
(cid:73) Under some limited conditions, it is possible a sorting in O(n)
(cid:73) Counting sort: the values are in an interval
(cid:73) Bucket sort: the values are approximately uniformly distributed
FII, UAIC
